Code fragment: public final TableField<AccRecord, String> TYPE = createField(DSL.name("type"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR(32).defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("'Default'"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR)), this, "The Type of the ACC. List: Default, Extension, AllExtension.");
Explanation: The TYPE column appears to store a limited set of predefined values (Default, Extension, AllExtension) directly in the column definition. This makes it difficult to add new types without modifying the schema. Consider creating a separate lookup table for ACC types and referencing it via a foreign key.

Code fragment: public final TableField<AccRecord, String> STATE = createField(DSL.name("state"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR(20).defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("NULL"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR)), this, "Deleted, WIP, Draft, QA, Candidate, Production, Release Draft, Published. This the revision life cycle state of the ACC.\n\nState change can't be undone. But the history record can still keep the records of when the state was changed.");
Explanation: The STATE column stores a limited set of lifecycle states (Deleted, WIP, Draft, QA, Candidate, Production, Release Draft, Published) as string values. This approach makes it harder to maintain valid states and add new ones. Consider creating a separate state lookup table and referencing it with a foreign key.
